problem_addressed: Calibration of machine learning models is brittle and easily lost under minimal covariate shifts between training and testing distributions.
core_claim: An importance sampling based approach can effectively calibrate models under covariate shift without requiring labeled target data.
key_contributions: Formal introduction of the problem of calibration under domain shift
Proposal of an importance sampling based approach to address calibration under covariate shift
Evaluation of the method on both real-world and synthetic datasets
novelty: Using importance sampling to adapt calibration loss from training to test distribution under covariate shift without requiring labeled target data.
method_summary: The method weights the calibration loss on training data by the density ratio γ(x) = πte(x)/πtr(x), enabling calibration under covariate shift without labeled target data.
method_components: Covariate shift assumption (πtr(X) ≠ πte(X) but πtr(Y|X) = πte(Y|X))
Importance sampling based weighting of calibration loss
Application to existing calibration techniques (like isotonic regression)
key_innovation: Theorem 4.1 proving that calibration loss under covariate shift is equivalent to density ratio weighted calibration loss on training data.
assumptions: Access to labeled training data from source distribution
Access to unlabeled test data from target distribution
Covariate shift assumption: πtr(X) ≠ πte(X) but πtr(Y|X) = πte(Y|X)
Support of test distribution includes support of training distribution (supp(πte) ⊃ supp(πtr))
theoretical_grounding: Theorem 4.1 mathematically proves that E(x,y)∼πte(X,Y)[l(h(x), y, ; β)] = E(x,y)∼πtr(X,Y)[γ(x)l(h(x), y, ; β)] where γ(x) = πte(x)/πtr(x)
stated_limitations: Existing techniques, including domain adaptation ones, primarily focus on prediction accuracy and do not guarantee calibration
Calibration is brittle and can be easily lost under minimal covariate shifts
unstated_weaknesses: No discussion of computational complexity or scalability for high-dimensional medical images
No analysis of sensitivity to proposal distribution choice in importance sampling
No consideration of class imbalance common in medical datasets
No evaluation of different types of covariate shifts
No ablation studies to isolate component contributions
threats_to_validity: Synthetic datasets may not adequately represent real-world medical imaging shifts
Limited generalizability assessment beyond evaluated domains
open_questions: How does method perform with feature vs. prior probability shifts?
Can importance sampling be optimized for high-dimensional medical images?
What is sensitivity to proposal distribution selection?
Does calibration maintain over continuous distribution shifts?
future_work: Extending to high-dimensional medical imaging data
gaps_this_enables: Direct pathway to calibration-aware active learning for medical images
Need for domain-specific calibration methods in medical imaging
Framework for integrating calibration into active learning pipelines
relevance_to_topic: Provides foundational approach for addressing calibration under covariate shifts, directly applicable to medical image classification where distribution shifts are common
limitations_stated: Existing techniques, including domain adaptation ones, primarily focus on prediction accuracy and do not guarantee calibration
Calibration is brittle and can be easily lost under minimal covariate shifts
title: Unsupervised Calibration under Covariate Shift
